verte.title: Verte
verte.subtitle: Nowhere's End Absinthe / Verte
verte.intro: A classic green absinthe: bitter, aromatic, bright, and deliberately free of minty modern distraction.
verte.story: Verte is the most traditional expression in the line, built around wormwood, anise, fennel, and classic coloring herbs for a vivid green louche and a focused aperitif ritual.
verte.effectTerritory: Bright, bitter, and lucidly herbal; built for a focused pre-ban Parisian aperitif ritual.
verte.serve: Serve 1:4 with cold water, no sugar or half sugar.
verte.flavor: Bitter green, classic louche, Roman wormwood, hyssop, melissa, coriander lift.
verte.marketingPhrase: For the green hour before dinner.

recipeSections.title: Primary botanical bill
items: Classic bitter backbone and absinthe identity.
Soft Artemisia detail in the base and the classic verte coloring finish.
Carries the sweet anisic body and the louche.
Rounds the body and keeps the sweetness botanical.
Provides resinous green altitude and classic coloration character.
Gives green citrus lift and aroma precision.
Acts as a root fixative and drying frame.

recipeSections.title: Claim-safe positioning
items: Use ritual language rather than saying stimulant, focus aid, or nootropic.
Aperitif and aromatic clarity are defensible; drug-effect language is not.
Do not imply enhanced cognition or stimulant function.

recipeSections.title: Extraction plan
items: Run grand wormwood, Roman wormwood, green anise, fennel, coriander, and angelica in the still for the structural absinthe spine.
Use Roman wormwood, hyssop, and lemon balm in a short controlled post-distillation maceration to set the verte color and green aromatic lift without stewing the batch.
Do not use mint or gentian in this expression; they pull the profile away from the pre-ban Parisian target.

herbs: classic coloring-herb signature and softer Artemisia nuance
sweet aromatic spine and louche support
fresh citron lift without breaking the style
resinous green top notes and medicinal altitude
molecules: sweet anisic body and louche behavior
dry fennel tension that keeps the sweetness trimmed
small floral lift that broadens the mid-palate
green coloration from the classic verte finish
serviceNotes: Talk about aperitif structure, attention to ritual, and green bitterness, not cognition claims.
The bottle should feel classically absinthe and specifically less mint-driven than a modern herbal liqueur.

rouge.title: Crimson
rouge.subtitle: Nowhere's End Absinthe / Crimson
rouge.intro: A crimson absinthe with floral tartness, warm spice, and a dramatic ruby pour.
rouge.story: Crimson keeps the absinthe spine intact while opening into hibiscus, rosehip, citrus, and rose for a vivid, table-centered serve.
rouge.effectTerritory: Tart, floral, crimson, and convivial; designed for a dramatic table ritual.
rouge.serve: Serve 1:3.5 with cold water and optional expressed orange peel.
rouge.flavor: Hibiscus, citrus, anise, warm spice, and floral bitter finish.
rouge.marketingPhrase: A crimson absinthe for the table.

recipeSections.title: Primary botanical bill
items: Creates a citrus bridge into hibiscus.
Lifts the top note with sparkling spice.

recipeSections.title: Claim-safe positioning
items: Use convivial and table-centered language rather than mood or heart-health claims.
Frame the bottle as a dramatic ritual for the table.
Do not imply cardiovascular or sexual-function benefits.

recipeSections.title: Extraction plan
items: Distill wormwoods, anise, fennel, star anise, coriander, orange peel, angelica, cardamom, and grains of paradise for a stable absinthe frame.
Do not drive hibiscus or rosehip through a hot still maceration; they are better handled outside the main distillation path to preserve fresh tart color.
Build the crimson layer with controlled cold extracts or finishing tinctures of hibiscus, rosehip, and trace rose petal, then bench the dose against louche strength and acid balance.

herbs: keeps the red build anchored to absinthe
citrus bridge to the red floral core
molecules: stops the profile from becoming plush
adds rose bloom and soft lift
serviceNotes: Position it as a vivid table ritual, not a physiological mood modifier.
The visual story should support conviviality without drifting into supplement language.

detour.title: Detour
detour.subtitle: Nowhere's End Absinthe / Detour
detour.intro: A less-licorice amber absinthe with sharp citrus, dry resin, gentian bitterness, and warm color.
detour.story: Detour steps away from the expected path, using sharp citrus peel, juniper, mastic, gentian, calendula, and dry root notes to pull the shared absinthe base away from licorice.
detour.effectTerritory: Less-licorice amber absinthe: sharp citrus, dry resin, bitter root, and warm color.
detour.serve: Serve 1:4 with cold water; garnish only with a restrained citrus peel if needed.
detour.flavor: Less licorice, more lemon and grapefruit peel, juniper, resin, gentian bitterness, and amber botanical warmth.
detour.marketingPhrase: The amber side road away from licorice.

recipeSections.title: Primary botanical bill
items: Keeps the expression dry, adult, and bitter.
Keeps a minimal louche grammar without leading the profile.
Supports texture while staying less licorice than the house base.
Pushes the top note toward clean citrus brightness.
Adds amber warmth without becoming sweet-orange cordial.
Adds bitter citrus complexity and suppresses licorice perception.
Grounds the airy top notes as a dry root fixative.
Gives resinous mouthfeel and ritual strangeness.
Supplies wild dry bitterness; keep as an approved editorial choice only.
Hardens the bitter axis and pulls the profile away from licorice.
Supports a warm amber color without blue/violet performance.

recipeSections.title: Claim-safe positioning
items: Use unexpected and less-traveled language rather than cognitive or psychedelic claims.
Make the flavor deviation itself the point.
Do not imply altered consciousness benefits.

recipeSections.title: Extraction plan
items: Run wormwoods, low anise, fennel, coriander, lemon peel, grapefruit peel, juniper, angelica, and trace rosemary in the still to establish the dry citrus-resin base.
If mastic is used as a resinous bridge, keep it to a very light post-distillation maceration or tincture addition so it reads as texture rather than turpentine.
Use gentian, calendula, and the final citrus adjustments as controlled finishing components; avoid butterfly pea and cornflower for this amber direction.

herbs: keeps the expression dry, adult, and bitter
molecules: keeps the citrus line bright and green
serviceNotes: Sell the amber side-road feeling and the less-licorice contrast, not altered consciousness.
Detour should read as unexpected but still dry, adult, and recognizably absinthe.

bleue.title: Bleue
bleue.subtitle: Nowhere's End Absinthe / Bleue
bleue.intro: A clear post-ban bootleg-style absinthe kept colorless for concealment, with no visible botanical finish.
bleue.story: Bleue is the clandestine post-ban expression: a clear absinthe kept colorless for concealment, built from the base distillation and finished without visible coloring botanicals.
bleue.effectTerritory: Clear, dry, illicit, and restrained; a smuggling-era blanche / la bleue profile.
bleue.serve: Serve 1:4.5 with very cold water and a slow drip.
bleue.flavor: Clear absinthe base, dry wormwood, anise-fennel louche, angelica root, and restrained citrus-spice brightness.
bleue.marketingPhrase: Clear enough to hide in plain sight.

recipeSections.title: Primary botanical bill
items: Keeps a soft Artemisia line inside the base distillation.
Keeps the center classically absinthe without decorative finish.
Makes the clear bottle round enough to louche gracefully.
Keeps the structure bright but not perfumed.
Creates cool rooty complexity and dry persistence.
Used only as uncolored reserve/proofing volume, not as a coloring maceration.

recipeSections.title: Claim-safe positioning
items: Use smuggling-era and clear ritual language rather than anxiety, sleep, or therapeutic claims.
The drama comes from history and concealment, not physiological effect.

recipeSections.title: Extraction plan
items: Distill wormwoods, anise, fennel, coriander, and angelica for a clear absinthe base.
Avoid green coloration maceration entirely; no hyssop, genepi, mint, elderflower, or cornflower finish in this expression.
Use clear base distillate reserve or proofing adjustment only, keeping the final profile colorless and spare.

herbs: soft Artemisia support in the base
molecules: holds the louche and sweet spice line
supports the dry root and spice frame
serviceNotes: Describe this as a clandestine post-ban clear absinthe, not a relaxant or sleep aid.
Its quiet should be historical and sensory: clear, dry, spare, and easy to conceal.
